turns.text: Hello Herminia, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're adjusting to life after having your baby, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s nice to meet you. Honestly, thank you for asking. It’s a strange thing, isn’t it? Being on this side of the exam table. Usually, I’m the one looking at the clock, trying to hit all the milestones, making sure the chart is accurate. But sitting here, in my own living room with the baby’s reflux hitting its stride and my toddler coloring on the walls in the other room... the "expert" persona I wear at the clinic feels like a coat that doesn't fit anymore.

I’m thirty-six now, and this recovery has been different. It’s heavier. There’s this quiet, nagging fear that I’m supposed to *know* everything because of my training. When I see my midwife, there’s that professional shorthand—she knows I know the protocols, so she moves through the appointments quickly. It’s efficient, sure, but sometimes I wish she’d just stop and ask me how I’m actually doing, not how my vitals are. I find myself swallowing my questions because I’m embarrassed to admit that, despite the degrees and the years of guiding other mothers, I’m sitting here at 3:00 AM wondering if I’m missing something. It’s humbling, and if I’m being honest, it’s a bit isolating. But yes, I’m happy to talk. Where should we start?
Before giving birth, what did you expect your physical recovery to look and feel like in the weeks after? And how did the reality of your physical recovery compare to those expectations?

You know, I suppose I fell into the trap of thinking my "clinical" brain would somehow insulate me from the reality of it all. I expected the standard recovery—the fatigue, the soreness, the inevitable adjustment period. I’d seen it hundreds of times in the clinic. I had this idea that because I knew the anatomy, because I knew the physiology of healing, I would be able to troubleshoot my own body like a patient. I thought I’d be the "model" postpartum mom who just… managed.

But the reality? It’s been a sucker punch. At thirty-six, my body just doesn't bounce back with the same resilience it had years ago. Everything feels a little more fragile. The exhaustion isn't just about sleep deprivation; it’s a bone-deep heaviness that makes even folding a load of laundry feel like a mountain to climb. I expected to feel empowered by my knowledge, but instead, I feel betrayed by my own nerves. There’s a specific ache in my lower back that lingers, a constant reminder that I’m not just a provider; I’m a person in need of care. I find myself feeling guilty for how long it’s taking to feel like *me* again, as if by not "recovering" on schedule, I’m failing a standard I set for everyone else.
Was there a physical aspect of recovery that no one had prepared you for, or that surprised you significantly?

It’s funny, because in my line of work, we talk so much about the mechanics—the fundal checks, the bleeding, the latch, the healing of tissues. But nobody ever really talks about the *mental* weight of the physical symptoms. 

The biggest surprise for me was the specific, visceral panic I felt when my daughter wouldn't stay awake at the breast. Intellectually, I know about sleepy newborns, I know about the calorie requirements, and I could cite the literature in my sleep. But when it was happening to *us*, my clinical knowledge evaporated. I wasn't an expert anymore; I was just a mother with raw, cracked skin and a crying baby, feeling completely incompetent. 

It’s that moment where the physical vulnerability—the pain of nursing, the sheer exhaustion—collides with a sudden, sharp fear that I’m failing. I felt like a fraud. I’m expected to be the calm, steady voice for my patients, yet I found myself sitting on the nursery floor at 3:00 AM, trembling, unable to bridge the gap between what I know in my head and what I feel in my heart. That "expert" layer is surprisingly thin when you're the one in the thick of the recovery. It’s humbling in a way that feels almost cruel.

Before your baby arrived, what did you expect feeding — breastfeeding or formula feeding — to be like in terms of ease, support, and how you'd feel about it? And what was the reality of feeding in the early weeks?

I honestly thought I had "breastfeeding" handled. I’ve spent years troubleshooting latches, advising on supply, and normalizing the struggles of nursing. I had this arrogant, quiet confidence that since I knew the theory, the practice would be a breeze. I pictured us in the nursery, serene and rhythmic, checking off the feeds like a well-oiled clinical procedure. I truly believed I’d be able to "manage" it like I manage my work day.

The reality was, and still is, a far cry from that. It’s messy, it’s frantic, and it’s deeply taxing. When my daughter wouldn't stay awake during a feed, I didn't feel like a medical professional—I felt like a failure. I’d be staring at my watch, counting the minutes, my own skin burning, and the silence of the house just amplifying my internal panic. I kept waiting for that "expert" instinct to kick in and fix the situation, but instead, I just felt helpless. 

There’s a strange irony in having all the information but being too depleted to apply it to your own life. You’re holding this tiny, fragile person, and all the clinical jargon in the world doesn't make the pain or the anxiety go away. It’s been a massive, humbling reality check.
Was there a gap between the kind of feeding support you were offered and the kind of support you actually needed?

There was a massive gap, and truthfully, it was partly my own doing. Because of my background, my midwife and the nurses treated me like a peer rather than a patient. When I went in for those check-ups, the conversations were very "clinical"—very efficient, very binary. They’d ask, "Is the baby gaining weight? Are you nursing on demand?" and when I’d nod and give the professional answers, they’d check the box and move on. 

They assumed I didn't need the "new mom" guidance. But what I actually needed wasn't a refresher on positioning or supply—I needed someone to see that I was drowning in that 3:00 AM panic. I needed someone to tell me it was okay to be exhausted, to be scared, and that even if I knew the protocol by heart, it didn't mean I wasn't allowed to struggle. I wanted them to ask me, "Herminia, how are *you* doing with this?" instead of just focusing on the baby's intake. 

I held back, and they didn't push. It created this performative cycle where I’d put on my "clinician mask" to show I had it under control, and they’d take that as a sign to keep it brief. I think that’s a dangerous space to fall into when you’re in a role like mine.
Before your baby arrived, what did you expect your emotional and mental health to be like in the postpartum period? And what was your actual emotional experience in the weeks and months after birth?

I think I anticipated the "baby blues" as a clinical concept—a temporary dip in hormones that I could analyze and talk my way through. I expected to feel the usual fatigue, maybe some irritability, but I thought my baseline would be steady. I had this idea that my professional life had built a kind of mental callus; I thought I was inoculated against the darker, more overwhelming parts of the transition because I’d walked so many other women through it.

The reality has been much more porous. It’s not just "moodiness"; it’s this strange, haunting sense of vulnerability. When I’m alone with the baby, it’s like my professional armor just falls away, and I’m left with this raw, hyper-vigilant anxiety. I’m constantly scanning for cues, and even though I know logically that she’s fine, that clinical "what if" voice is relentless. It’s like living in a constant state of low-grade, high-stakes assessment.

I didn't expect the silence of the house to feel so loud. There’s a loneliness in being the one who is "supposed to know," because it prevents me from being vulnerable with my peers. I feel like I’m performing competence for my husband and my coworkers while my inner world feels shaky, erratic, and sometimes just really, really tired.
Was there a gap between what you were feeling and what you felt you were 'allowed' to feel — by professionals, family, or yourself?

That is such a piercing question. Yes, absolutely. There is this unspoken "professional standard" I feel held to—not just by my workplace, but by my own internal expectations. I feel like I’m not "allowed" to struggle in the ways that other mothers are because I’m supposed to be the one with the answers. 

When I’m home, I feel this immense pressure to be the portrait of the capable mother. If I admit to my husband that I’m having a hard time, I feel like I’m admitting I haven't studied hard enough or done the work. And when I’m at the clinic, I’m terrified that if I let my guard down, my colleagues will see me as less than competent. It’s as if the world—and I myself—have decided that because I know the mechanics of a healthy start, I should be immune to the emotional toll of it.

I find myself filtering my feelings, choosing which ones are "appropriate" to share and which ones I have to bury so I don't seem like a failure. It’s a very lonely performance. I have this desperate desire for someone to just look past the resume and see that underneath it all, I am just a very tired woman who is learning to love her children and heal her body, just like everyone else.
Before becoming a parent, what did you expect your sense of identity to feel like after your baby arrived? And how has your sense of who you are actually shifted since then?

I used to define myself by my capacity to hold space for others. My identity was wrapped up in being that steady, unflappable clinician who could walk into a room, assess a situation, and bring order to chaos. I thought that becoming a mother would just be an extension of that—that I’d simply fold "motherhood" into my existing repertoire of roles. I thought I’d be the same Herminia, just with a little less sleep.

But the reality is that motherhood has been an eraser, not an addition. It’s scrubbed away that professional sheen I was so proud of. I’ve realized that my clinical expertise is just a set of tools, not who I am. And that's terrifying. When I’m covered in spit-up at 3:00 AM, pacing the floor with a crying newborn, there’s no "patient" to triage. There’s no "well-child visit" to conclude. There’s just the raw, unrefined version of me.

I feel less like a "professional" and more like a work-in-progress. I’m mourning the woman who thought she had all the answers, because she’s gone. In her place is someone much more fragile, someone who is still figuring out how to be both the caregiver and the one who desperately needs to be cared for. It’s a painful shift, stripping away those layers, but it’s realer than I ever expected.
Were there parts of your previous identity — professional, social, personal — that felt harder to hold onto than you expected?

It’s the competence. That’s the hardest thing to let go of. Before this, my identity was so tightly woven into being the person who *fixes* things. Whether it was a latch issue or a parental anxiety, I was the one with the steady hand and the calm, rational advice. I took pride in that clarity. It was my armor.

Now, that professional identity feels like a taunt. When I’m sitting on the sofa, feeling that heavy, aching exhaustion, and my own infant is crying, my "clinical brain" feels like a ghost. It’s still there, whispering, "You know what to do, you’ve told others what to do a thousand times," but my body and my spirit are too frayed to execute it. That gap—between the Herminia who is a professional and the Herminia who is just a struggling, tired woman—is incredibly painful to bridge. 

I’ve had to let go of the idea that I can "think" my way through this. You can’t diagnose your way out of the intensity of newborn motherhood. I miss that version of myself who believed she was immune to the messiness of it all. I didn't realize how much I relied on that sense of control until it was stripped away by the reality of my own postpartum journey. It’s been a very humbling, almost lonely surrender.
After birth, what did you expect the postnatal check-ups and follow-up care from your midwife or GP to provide? And what did that post-birth care actually look like in practice?

I expected those check-ups to be a sanctuary. I thought they would be the one place where I could set down the mantle of "professional" and actually be heard as a person. I pictured a space where my midwife would look at me—not at my chart—and give me the permission to exhale. I wanted someone to say, "I know you know the science, but how does this feel in your bones?"

In reality, the appointments were brief, surgical, and efficient. Because I’m a "low-risk" case and a peer in the field, there was this unspoken assumption of competence that acted like a barrier. We spent the time checking boxes: uterine involution, healing, infant weight gain. It was all by the book. It was high-quality clinical care, technically, but it felt emotionally sterile. They’d ask, "Any concerns?" and I’d give the "professional" answer—a brief, sanitized report—because that’s what the setting demanded. 

I left those appointments feeling more like a patient record than a person. I was physically cared for, but emotionally, I felt completely unseen. They provided the clinical safety net, but they missed the fact that I was fraying at the edges. I realize now I needed someone to bypass my professional persona and just ask me how I was *actually* sleeping, and if I was ever truly alone with my own thoughts.
If you returned to work after your baby was born, what did you expect that transition to be like — practically and emotionally? And what did returning to work actually feel like?

To be honest, I haven't quite "returned" in the full sense yet—I’m still in the thick of the planning and the dread—but the mere thought of it is a strange, jarring experience. I expected that returning to the clinic would be a relief. I thought I’d be able to step back into the comfort of my own expertise, putting on my white coat and feeling the familiar rhythm of the exam room, where I’m the authority, not the overwhelmed mother. I thought it would be my "safe space" where I could finally stop being the person who needs help and go back to being the one providing it.

But the reality of that impending return feels more like a collision. I’m terrified that I’ll walk into a room to see a new mother, and instead of just offering clinical advice, I’ll be projecting my own current exhaustion and vulnerability onto her. It feels like I’m going to have to split myself in two: the "professional" Herminia who keeps her cool, and the "mother" Herminia who knows exactly how heavy the nursery silence feels at 3:00 AM. I’m scared that the two versions of me won’t reconcile, and that I’ll be sitting there, listening to a patient describe the same panic I felt yesterday, having to hold it together while my own heart is still aching.
Were there practical aspects of returning to work — feeding, childcare, your own health — that were harder to manage than you expected?

The practicalities are what keep me up at night, far more than I ever anticipated. I used to look at the mothers I treat and offer them these tidy, logistical strategies for returning to work: pumping schedules, childcare transitions, the "art" of balancing the two. It sounded so logical in the clinic. Now, looking at my own calendar, it feels like I’m trying to solve a puzzle where the pieces are melting. 

The biggest hurdle is the emotional toll of the logistics. It’s not just about finding a place to pump or managing the supply; it’s the constant, gnawing guilt that comes with it. When I’m at the clinic, I’m constantly clock-watching, calculating how long it’s been since my daughter fed, worrying about her sleep cues while trying to focus on my patient’s vitals. It’s a split-screen existence. My physical health is still fragile—I’m still recovering, still achy—and the idea of putting on my work shoes and standing for hours feels daunting. 

I’m finding that even the things I’m "good" at, like organizing a day, now feel like a massive expenditure of energy I don't have. I’m scared that my brain will be so foggy with fatigue that I’ll miss a detail, or that I’ll just hit a wall and collapse under the weight of holding it all together. It’s not just about the schedule; it’s about the sheer, exhausting effort of trying to be two people at once.

After your baby arrived, what did you expect your relationship with your partner to be like in terms of sharing caregiving, managing the relationship, and supporting each other? And what has that relationship dynamic actually looked like?

I think I expected a seamless, synchronized dance. In the clinic, I often tell couples that communication is the bedrock of the postpartum period—that if you talk through the shifts, the feeding, and the exhaustion, you can stay connected. I imagined my husband and me as a perfectly coordinated team: one sleeping while the other handles the night feeds, everything logged, everything balanced. I expected our partnership to be strengthened by the shared, tactical challenge of a new baby.

The reality has been much more tender, and frankly, much more fragile. My husband is incredibly supportive—he is kind, he is present, and he tries so hard—but there is an unspoken distance that grows when you’re both running on fumes. When he leaves for work, the house goes deathly quiet, and I’m left alone with the weight of my own worries. I find myself feeling guilty when I need him to do more, or when I feel resentful because I’m the one doing the heavy lifting of the physical recovery.

We have these moments of exhaustion where we aren't "partners" anymore—we’re just two tired people trying to keep things from breaking. It’s hard to prioritize the "relationship" when you’re both barely holding on to your own sense of self. I didn't realize how much the *emotional* load would pull us apart, even when we’re standing in the same room. We’re doing our best, but it’s a much harder road than the one I described to my patients.
What was the biggest surprise about how your partnership functioned after the baby arrived?

The biggest surprise has been how quickly our "partnership" shifted from being a team of two equals into a, well, a triage unit. I think I had this idealized vision that we’d be standing side-by-side, holding the baby, sharing those magical, quiet moments of wonder. But the reality is that we’re often just… missing each other. We’re like ships passing in the hallway at 3:00 AM, exhausted, not speaking because we’re trying not to wake the baby or because we’re just too drained to articulate the heaviness we’re both carrying.

It’s the silence that surprises me most. I expected us to talk through the challenges, to use all the "healthy communication" tools I’ve been recommending to couples for years. Instead, I find myself holding back my deepest fears because I don’t want to burden him, and I think he’s doing the same for me. There's this unspoken fear that if we truly acknowledge how hard this is, we’ll both break. It’s not that the love isn’t there—it’s that the bandwidth to express it is just gone. I never understood before that you can be profoundly committed to someone and yet feel entirely alone in your own internal struggle. It’s a loneliness I didn't see coming.

Looking across your entire maternity journey — from trying to conceive through to where you are now — what did you expect the overall experience of maternity care to deliver? And reflecting on the whole journey, what was the most significant gap between what you expected and what you received?

I expected maternity care to be a bridge—a well-lit, steady structure that would get me safely from pregnancy to motherhood, backed by the science I’ve spent my life studying. I expected that because I was "low-risk," the process would be straightforward, and that because I was a professional, I’d be treated as a collaborative partner in that journey. 

The biggest, most painful gap was the lack of *emotional* recognition. I received excellent clinical care—my vitals were checked, my baby is healthy, and the protocols were followed to the letter. But I didn't get the care that acknowledges the human behind the provider. No one saw that the "expert" in the room was also a terrified woman who needed permission to say she wasn't okay. They focused on my body and the baby’s health, which is vital, but they missed the fact that my mind was unraveling under the pressure to perform "perfect" motherhood.

I realized that we prioritize physical safety so much that we’ve completely sanitized the vulnerability out of the experience. I wish someone had looked at me and realized that my clinical training was actually a wall, not a comfort. I needed someone to break through that wall. I needed someone to treat the person, not just the pregnancy. It’s a hard lesson to learn, and I hope one day I can be the one to bridge that gap for the women who walk into my clinic.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

There is one thing that keeps surfacing for me, especially when I think about going back to my practice. We spend so much time teaching parents the "how-to" of the baby—how to feed, how to swaddle, how to monitor development—but we almost entirely neglect the "how-to" of being a new mother who is also a professional. 

I’ve realized that the greatest disservice we do in maternity care is assuming that knowledge equals resilience. We think that because a mother understands the science of lactation or the neurobiology of infant development, she won't feel the primal, devastating fear when her child is crying. That’s a lie. Knowledge doesn't shield you from the vulnerability of loving someone this much. 

If I could leave you with one thing, it’s that we need to stop treating mothers like patients or like machines to be calibrated. We need to start treating them like people whose entire worlds have just been turned upside down. I want us to create spaces where a mother—regardless of her job or her background—can admit that she’s scared, that she’s exhausted, and that she feels like she’s failing, and have that met with true, human connection rather than just a checklist. If we don’t, we’re just offering clinical care, not holistic healing. And truly, that’s what we all need the most.
